stem: The curve \( y=[[a]]x^{2}+([[b]])x \) has a tangent line at \( x=[[x0]] \). What is the slope of the NORMAL line there?
solution: Tangent slope \( =2\cdot[[a]]\cdot[[x0]]+[[b]] \); the normal slope is its negative reciprocal \( [[answer]] \).

stem: At what \( x \)-value does the curve \( y=[[p]]x^{2}+[[q]]x+[[r]] \) have a horizontal tangent line?
solution: A horizontal tangent occurs where \( f'(x)=2\cdot[[p]]x+[[q]]=0 \), so \( x=-\dfrac{[[q]]}{2\cdot[[p]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the negative sign that comes from moving \( q \) to the other side.
This forgets to divide by 2 when isolating \( x \).
This confuses the coefficients, multiplying instead of dividing.
This reports the coefficient \( q \) itself instead of solving for \( x \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The curve \( y=x^{3}+([[b]])x \) has horizontal tangent lines at some values of \( x \). Which of the following is one such value?
solution: A horizontal tangent occurs where \( f'(x)=3x^{2}+([[b]])=0 \), i.e. \( x^{2}=[[k]]^{2} \), so \( x=\pm [[k]] \); \( x=[[answer]] \) is one such value.
distractor_rationales: This assumes a horizontal tangent always occurs at the origin.
This reports the OTHER valid horizontal-tangent value, not this one (both are correct, but only one can be the keyed answer here).
This forgets to take a square root, using \( 3k \) instead of \( k \).
This forgets to take a square root, using \( -3k \) instead of \( -k \).
This is off by one from a correct value.
This is off by one from a correct value.

stem: The circle \( x^{2}+y^{2}=[[R]] \) has a vertical tangent line at some values of \( x \). Which of the following is one such value?
solution: A vertical tangent occurs where \( \frac{dy}{dx} \) is undefined, i.e. where \( y=0 \); substituting into \( x^{2}+y^{2}=[[R]] \) gives \( x^{2}=[[R]] \), so \( x=\pm [[rv]] \); \( x=[[answer]] \) is one such value.
distractor_rationales: This assumes a vertical tangent always occurs at the origin.
This reports \( R \) itself instead of \( \pm\sqrt{R} \).
This doubles the radius instead of taking the square root of \( R \).
This reports the OTHER valid vertical-tangent value, not this one (both are correct, but only one can be the keyed answer here).
This is off by one from a correct value.
This is off by one from a correct value.

stem: The curve \( [[a]]x^{2}+[[b]]y^{2}=[[C]] \) has horizontal tangent lines at some points. Which of the following is a possible \( y \)-coordinate of such a point?
solution: Implicit differentiation gives \( \frac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{[[a]]x}{[[b]]y} \), which vanishes when \( x=0 \); substituting into the curve gives \( [[b]]y^{2}=[[C]] \), so \( y=\pm [[m]] \); \( y=[[answer]] \) is one such value.
distractor_rationales: This assumes a horizontal tangent always occurs at \( y=0 \).
This reports \( C \) itself instead of solving for \( y \).
This doubles the correct magnitude instead of solving \( by^{2}=C \).
This reports the OTHER valid \( y \)-coordinate, not this one (both are correct, but only one can be the keyed answer here).
This is off by one from a correct value.
This is off by one from a correct value.

stem: The circle \( x^{2}+y^{2}=[[r2]] \) passes through \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \). What is the slope of the NORMAL line to the circle at that point?
solution: Implicit differentiation gives the tangent slope \( \frac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{x}{y} \), so at \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) the tangent slope is \( -\dfrac{[[x0]]}{[[y0]]} \); the normal slope is its negative reciprocal, \( \dfrac{[[y0]]}{[[x0]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the TANGENT slope instead of the normal slope.
This inverts the correct ratio.
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This multiplies the coordinates instead of dividing them.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The NORMAL line to \( y=[[p]]x^{2}+([[q]])x+([[r]]) \) at \( x=[[x0]] \) crosses the \( y \)-axis at what value of \( y \)?
solution: The tangent slope is \( m=2\cdot[[p]]([[x0]])+[[q]] \), so the normal slope is \( -1/m \); the normal line \( y=f([[x0]])-\tfrac{1}{m}(x-[[x0]]) \) has \( y \)-intercept \( f([[x0]])+\dfrac{[[x0]]}{m} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the correction term instead of adding it.
This uses the TANGENT line's y-intercept formula instead of the normal line's.
This reports \( f([[x0]]) \), the function value, instead of the normal line's \( y \)-intercept.
This reports only the correction term, forgetting the base value \( f(x_0) \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
